utterances: Forced himself out of the party.
Well, also his own ideology, and his dare to embrace President Obama when he came down to Florida for the stimulus really turned off a lot of conservatives.
But so Bill McCollum was this, you know, establishment figure, state attorney general, former congressman, ran twice for the Senate.

But Rick Scott, who is, again, a millionaire, spent about $50 million of his own money there's a lot of money being spent in gubernatorial races this year, as Meg Whitman will tell you in California and - but Rick Scott has his own problems.
He was a CEO of a hospital chain that was - paid $1.7 billion in fines because of Medicare fraud.
And this could help the Democratic nominee, Alex Sink.
She'd be the first female governor of Florida.
But as in the Senate race, there was a third candidate running - Lawton Chiles, the last Democratic governor of Florida.
His son, Lawton III - or Bud Chiles, as we call him - is running as an independent, and that could hurt the Democrats.
So we'll have to see how that works out.
Quickly to Arizona, before we get to people who think they know the answer to this week's trivia question.
And in Arizona, thought for the Republican gubernatorial bid, well, that might have been a contest earlier.
It turned out not to be: Jan Brewer.
Right.
Jan Brewer won big.

Once she signed the 1070, the controversial anti-illegal-immigration law, the Republican opposition to her basically disappeared, and she is now the clear favorite in November against Terry Goddard, the state attorney general, son of a former governor.
And she outlined some of her campaign platform last night in her victory speech.
Sydney Freedburg, thank you for coming in to talk to us.
Mr. FREEDBURG JR.: Thank you.
This general election is going to be about very simple choices.
Either you are embraced and supported by organizations who boycott our state, or you work for the people of Arizona and the right for the truth to be heard.
And that, of course, referring to the boycotts of Arizona that have been sponsored by some, and of course if you're opposing her, that means you're supporting - anyway, you get the picture.
It looks like this could be a tough race.
It could be, and obviously illegal immigration, or immigration, will be a key issue, as it usually is in Arizona.
And of course, you have the Senate race now, which is basically, the whole issue was whether John McCain would be vulnerable or not.
It turned out he was not.
He beat J.D.
Hayworth, the first challenge of McCain's long Senate career, beat him easily in the primary.

In Vermont, no big surprise on the Democratic senatorial side.
Patrick Leahy sailed to re-nomination, no big surprise there.
But a tight race there for the gubernatorial nomination.
One quick thing about Pat Leahy.
It was interesting.
When he was first elected to the Senate in 1974, he was the first Democrat ever elected to the Senate from Vermont.
Now he's seeking his seventh term.
It's just amazing how things in politics have changed over the decades in Vermont.
In the gubernatorial race, the Republican governor, Jim Douglas, is not running again.
His lieutenant governor, Brian Dubie, is running again, is running for governor.
But there in a five-way race...
There's a pun - it couldn't be dumpy(ph).
No, it's Dubie.
And you know, there's a song - I think Frank Sinatra had a song about him.
Did.

Exactly.
Peter Shumlin, who is the president of the state senate, leads by 182 votes over the second-place finisher, Doug Racine.
And the third-place finisher, Deb Markowitz, is down by 738 votes.
Anytime you want to know whether your vote counts, it certainly did yesterday in Vermont's Democratic primary.
And I think Shumlin has declared victory.
The others have yet to concede.

But briefly, Ken, that is going to be one of the stunning political events of the primary season.
You know, I mean, we talked from the beginning; there's this guy, Joe Miller.
Nobody ever heard of him.
He was a Gulf War veteran - he is a Gulf War veteran, Tea Party favorite.
Sarah Palin endorsed him.

But again, Lisa Murkowski had the money, had the establishment, and basically the feeling in Alaska was what we the fact that we get so much more largesse from the federal government than we give in, that's the way Ted Ted Williams...

Ted Stevens did it.
That's the way Murkowski did it.
That's the way we like it.
And nobody thought that Joe Miller had a shot.
And right now, he's leading by 1,960 votes.

Well, it's absolutely definite that her endorsement of this campaign has helped us.
